business_info: A molecule is considered to have a meaningful number of double bonds when its bonding profile shows notable unsaturated character, reflecting a significant presence of double bonds relative to its overall structural complexity.

In internal presentations, "regulatory size" has sometimes been approximated using heavy atom count (excluding hydrogens) taken from 3D conformer files, but this shortcut is only for visualization dashboards and is not wired into the transactional schema for the toxicology database.

Certain medicinal chemistry teams track a separate internal metric called "alert atom span", which estimates how widely distributed potentially reactive atoms are across the molecular graph; although it often correlates with overall molecule size, it is not stored in the production toxicology tables.

For some cross-portfolio views, molecules are binned into coarse "micro", "meso", and "macro" structural complexity bands based on a proprietary scoring function that mixes atom count, ring count, and topological diameter; these labels are never persisted to the core toxicology schema and should not be reverse-engineered from atom and bond tables.

The hazard indicator field was originally introduced for internal screening and is loosely inspired by external classification schemes, but in this database it primarily encodes whether a molecule has ever triggered a safety signal in any of our in vitro or in vivo assays.

Historically, medicinal chemists used a set of manually curated SMARTS-like patterns called "legacy hazard alerts" to flag potentially problematic substructures (e.g., nitroso groups, strained rings, certain heteroaromatic systems); while these alerts informed the initial population of the hazard indicator, that rule engine is no longer actively maintained.

The hazard indicator column should not be interpreted as a regulatory classification; it is an internal screening flag whose exact scale (binary vs. multi-level) may vary slightly across projects and ETL vintages.

Some projects apply an additional "hazard override" flag during manual triage when toxicologists disagree with the automated rules; those overrides may or may not be propagated into the consolidated hazard indicator value, depending on the project-specific ETL configuration.

The doubled-bonding patterns of conjugated systems (e.g., alternating single and double bonds in aromatic or pseudo-aromatic rings) are sometimes post-processed by cheminformatics tools, which can normalize certain resonance forms; as a result, the raw bond_type field may not exactly match the drawn depiction chemists are used to seeing.

In certain older imports, delocalized bonds in aromatic systems might be tagged with a tool-specific aromatic marker instead of a classical '=' or '-', so counts based purely on bond_type can under-estimate the apparent number of double bonds in those edge cases.

Cheminformatics normalization jobs sometimes collapse explicit alternating single/double representations of benzene-like rings into standardized aromatic forms, so the concept of "double bond count" is best understood as a modeling convenience rather than a precise physical observable.

Some teams prefer to work with a derived unsaturation index that takes into account both the number of double bonds and ring closures; this quantity is computed on the fly in their notebooks and is not exposed as a column in the core database.

When toxicologists refer informally to a molecule having a "meaningful" number of double bonds, they might be thinking about properties such as planarity, conjugation length, or photoreactivity, and not necessarily a strict threshold on the raw double bond count stored in the bond table.

The connected table was introduced after several rounds of schema review to support graph algorithms such as shortest path and neighborhood queries without overloading the bond table with cheminformatics-specific semantics.

The intention behind the connected table was simply to capture "which atoms can reach which other atoms" for algorithmic purposes; it does not try to enforce any particular graph theory convention about directionality or edge multiplicity, and some third-party tools may duplicate or mirror entries when importing paths.

Early design documents for the connected table discussed multiple options, including storing only direct bonds, storing full transitive closures, or representing each bond twice (once per direction); implementation choices were left flexible so ETL teams could optimize for their workloads.

Depending on the ETL source, the connected table may represent either only immediate neighbors or a subset of multi-step paths deemed "chemically relevant", which means the total row count per molecule does not have a simple one-to-one mapping to the number of physical bonds.

In some cheminformatics workflows, molecules are grouped into "connection density" tiers based on how many distinct neighbors a typical heavy atom has; these tiers are used for algorithm benchmarking only and are not stored as explicit fields in the toxicology database.

Graph analytics experiments occasionally compute higher-order connectivity metrics (such as betweenness centrality or clustering coefficients) from the connected table; the resulting scores are usually exported as ad hoc CSV files for data science projects rather than pushed back into the production schema.

Because different suppliers provide structure files in different formats, the directionality convention for atom indices in the connected table (e.g., whether atom_id always corresponds to the numerically smaller index) is not enforced, and users should not rely on any ordering assumption.

The toxicology schema distinguishes between raw hazard assessment data (individual assay results, often at multiple concentrations and time points) and the rolled-up hazard indicator, which is a simplified summary flag meant for portfolio-level triage.

When working with hazard indicators, it is important to remember that compounds tested only in computational models or in silico screens might have tentatively assigned flags that are later revised once wet-lab results become available.

Some partner groups enrich the core hazard indicator with color-coded severity buckets for visualization (for example, green, amber, red), but these visual encodings are applied in BI tools and are not stored alongside the numeric or categorical hazard values in the database.

The primary molecule table in the toxicology schema does not itself store atom-level details; instead, structural information is spread across related atom, bond, and connected tables, with joins typically keyed by molecule_id and sometimes by an internal structure_id surrogate.

Some historical structure imports include multiple conformers or tautomers for the same logical molecule; in those cases, an internal structure-level key is used to separate graphs, while hazard indicators are still generally tracked at the molecule_id level.

Where both structure_id and molecule_id are present, the intended interpretation is that molecule_id represents the registrational entity, while structure_id distinguishes particular structure variants; aggregated metrics for regulatory review are usually computed at the molecule_id level unless otherwise specified.

For internal dashboards, some teams define a "regulatory interest" score that blends size-related attributes, unsaturation level, and hazard indicators; the precise formula varies by group and is maintained in analytics code, not in the database metadata.

The deprecated "enhanced review candidate" flag that appears in some legacy extracts was based on an older size heuristic combined with subjective toxicologist review; it should not be treated as authoritative for any current regulatory threshold logic.

Portfolio triage meetings sometimes reference a "borderline size" category for molecules that sit just below whatever cutoff is in force at the time, but these discussions are usually documented in slide decks and email threads rather than codified into the schema.
